questions.text: I hold the view that the sincerity of my moral commitments is most accurately measured by how I respond to suffering, not by what I say about it.
I tend to describe my emotional experiences in moderate terms — things are good, fine, okay.
I respond to messages when the timing feels right, not according to a self-imposed schedule.
I think that the capacity for objective assessment in the presence of human pain is a form of strength that serves the common good.
I often end up as the reliable supporter in group projects.
I believe that my willingness to decide quickly is valuable to those around me.
I sense when someone chooses a specific word because another word would reveal too much.
I often realize only later that someone was upset during a conversation.
I find purpose in being the one who shoulders the burden so others don't have to.
I let the boundaries between different areas of my life blur naturally.
I find it natural to ask others what they expect from themselves.
After a stressful event, it takes me a long time to return to my emotional baseline.
I feel that my personal comfort and satisfaction are things I should actively cultivate.
I hold the view that the strongest contribution I can make starts with a strong self.
I experience emotions at a moderate volume — present but never overwhelming.
I find that I navigate emotionally complex situations more effectively by treating them as intellectual problems with emotional dimensions, rather than the reverse.
I take a methodical approach to learning new skills, following a structured curriculum.
I find that I am usually the last person to notice when the mood in a room has shifted.
